body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
body.name: PseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
source.label: Definition 1

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
source.label: Definition 2

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g).
source.label: Definition 3

body.text: In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).

body.label: Definition 8
body.definition: An abelian group A is torsion-free if T(A)={0}.
body.name: IsTorsionFreeAbelianGroup

source.text: An abelian group A is torsion-free if T(A)={0}.
source.label: Definition 8

proof.text: Fix x,y in G. We first prove l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n). The group identity (yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1} gives n l(yxy^{-1}) = l(yx^n y^{-1}). By the triangle inequality, l(yx^n y^{-1}) <= l(y) + l(x^n) + l(y^{-1}). By symmetry, l(y^{-1})=l(y). By homogeneity, l(x^n)=n l(x). Hence n l(yxy^{-1}) <= 2l(y)+n l(x). Dividing by the positive real number n gives l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x)+2l(y)/n. Since this inequality holds for every positive integer n, and 2l(y)/n tends to 0, it follows that l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). For the reverse inequality, define z := yxy^{-1}. Then x=y^{-1}zy. Applying the inequality already proved with z in place of x and y^{-1} in place of y gives l(x)=l(y^{-1}zy) <= l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}). The two inequalities imply l(yxy^{-1})=l(x).

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Then for every x,y in G, l(yxy^{-1}) = l(x).
source.label: Lemma 1

body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (w y z s t : G) (n : ℕ), let C : ℕ → G := fun n => (w * y) ^ n * s⁻¹ * t * (z * w⁻¹) ^ n; l (C n) ≤ l (s⁻¹) + l t + n * (l y + l z)

proof_steps.proof_method: By the induction hypothesis.

Algebraic rearrangement and collecting terms.
side_conditions: Addition is associative and commutative in the codomain of l.

induction_step_proof.text: Assume the inequality holds for a natural number n. The definition gives C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}. Equivalently, C_{n+1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}. By Lemma 1, applied to the element yC_nz and the conjugating element w, l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz). By two applications of the triangle inequality, l(yC_nz) <= l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z). By the induction hypothesis, l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Combining the last two inequalities gives l(C_{n+1}) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)). This proves the induction step.

proof.text: We prove the claim by induction on n. For n=0, the definition gives C_0=s^{-1}t. By the triangle inequality, l(C_0)=l(s^{-1}t) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t). This is the desired inequality for n=0. Assume the inequality holds for a natural number n. The definition gives C_{n+1}=wy C_n z w^{-1}. Equivalently, C_{n+1}=w(y C_n z)w^{-1}. By Lemma 1, applied to the element yC_nz and the conjugating element w, l(C_{n+1})=l(yC_nz). By two applications of the triangle inequality, l(yC_nz) <= l(y)+l(C_n)+l(z). By the induction hypothesis, l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Combining the last two inequalities gives l(C_{n+1}) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+(n+1)(l(y)+l(z)). This proves the induction step. Therefore the inequality holds for every natural number n.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Let w,y,z,s,t in G. For every natural number n, define C_n := (wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n. Then l(C_n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)).
source.label: Lemma 2

proof.text: Let n be a positive integer. From the two hypotheses and the standard identity for powers of conjugates, we have a^n=s(wy)^n s^{-1} and a^n=t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. Hence a^{2n}=a^n a^n =s(wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n t^{-1}. By homogeneity, 2n l(a)=l(a^{2n}). Using the displayed expression for a^{2n} and the triangle inequality, l(a^{2n}) <= l(s)+l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n)+l(t^{-1}). By Lemma 2, l((wy)^n s^{-1}t(zw^{-1})^n) <= l(s^{-1})+l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). By symmetry, l(s^{-1})=l(s) and l(t^{-1})=l(t). Therefore 2n l(a) <= 2l(s)+2l(t)+n(l(y)+l(z)). Dividing by 2n gives l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2+(l(s)+l(t))/n. Since this inequality holds for every positive integer n, and (l(s)+l(t))/n tends to 0, we obtain l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Let a,w,y,z,s,t in G. Assume a=s(wy)s^{-1} and a=t(zw^{-1})t^{-1}. Then l(a) <= (l(y)+l(z))/2.
source.label: Lemma 3

body.label: Lemma 4
body.name: lemma_4
body.claim: ∀ (G : Type*) [Group G] (l : G → ℝ), IsHomogeneousPseudoLength G l → ∀ (x y : G), let c : G := x * y * x⁻¹ * y⁻¹; let f : ℤ → ℤ → ℝ := fun m k => l (x ^ m * c ^ k); ∀ (m k : ℤ), f m k ≤ (f (m - 1) k + f (m + 1) (k - 1)) / 2

Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function
Fix x,y in G and set c:=[x,y]

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definitions of w, u, and a, and the power law x * x^(m-1) = x^m.

proof_steps.proof_method: Since a = w * u, equality gives conjugacy with conjugating element e.
proof_steps.source_claim: Thus a is conjugate to wu, because a=wu.

proof_steps.proof_method: By substituting w = x into v * w^(-1).
Using the commutator identity c = x y x^(-1) y^(-1), multiply on the right by y.
proof_steps.source_claim: Using the commutator identity c=xyx^{-1}y^{-1}, we have xyx^{-1}=cy.

proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite x y x^(-1) as c y, combine c^(k-1) c = c^k, and use the definition of a.
Rearrange the equality v * w^(-1) = y^(-1) a y by conjugating with y.

proof_steps.proof_method: The displayed equality gives conjugacy with conjugating element y.
proof_steps.source_claim: so a is conjugate to vw^{-1}.

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 3 with s = e, t = y, y = u, and z = v, using the two conjugacy statements just proved.
proof_steps.source_claim: Lemma 3, applied with s=e, t=y, y=u, and z=v, gives l(a)<= (l(u)+l(v))/2.

deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 3
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied with s = e, t = y, y = u, and z = v.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of u and the definition of f.
proof_steps.source_claim: By the definition of u, l(u)=l(x^{m-1}c^k)=f(m-1,k).

proof_steps.proof_method: Expand v = y^(-1) x^m c^(k-1) x y and cancel y * y^(-1) and y * y^(-1) at the ends.
Use associativity, cancellations, and the power law x * x^m = x^(m+1).

proof_steps.claim: v is conjugate to x^(m+1) c^(k-1)
proof_steps.proof_method: The first equality conjugates v to x^m c^(k-1) x by y, and the second conjugates that element to x^(m+1) c^(k-1) by x.
proof_steps.source_claim: The element v is conjugate to x^{m+1}c^{k-1} because ...

proof_steps.proof_method: Apply Lemma 1 to the conjugacy between v and x^(m+1) c^(k-1).
proof_steps.source_claim: By Lemma 1, conjugate elements have the same length.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For every x and y in G, l(y x y^(-1)) = l(x).
deduced_from_theorem.name: Lemma 1
deduced_from_theorem.description: Conjugate elements have the same length.

proof_steps.proof_method: Use the previous equality and the definition of f.

proof_steps.claim: f applied to m and k <= (f applied to (m - 1) and k + f applied to (m + 1) and (k - 1)) / 2
proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute the formulas for l(a), l(u), and l(v) into the inequality from Lemma 3, using the definition of f for l(a).
proof_steps.source_claim: Substituting the formulas for l(u) and l(v) into the inequality from Lemma 3 gives f(m,k)<= (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.

proof.text: Fix m,k in Z. Define a:=x^m c^k, w:=x, u:=x^{m-1}c^k, and v:=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xy. Then wu=x(x^{m-1}c^k)=x^m c^k=a. Thus a is conjugate to wu, because a=wu. We next prove that a is conjugate to vw^{-1}. Since w=x, vw^{-1}=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}xyx^{-1}. Using the commutator identity c=xyx^{-1}y^{-1}, we have xyx^{-1}=cy. Therefore vw^{-1}=y^{-1}x^m c^{k-1}cy=y^{-1}x^m c^k y=y^{-1}ay. Hence a=y(vw^{-1})y^{-1}, so a is conjugate to vw^{-1}. Lemma 3, applied with s=e, t=y, y=u, and z=v, gives l(a)<= (l(u)+l(v))/2. By the definition of u, l(u)=l(x^{m-1}c^k)=f(m-1,k). It remains to identify l(v). The element v is conjugate to x^{m+1}c^{k-1} because yvy^{-1}=x^m c^{k-1}x and x(x^m c^{k-1}x)x^{-1}=x^{m+1}c^{k-1}. By Lemma 1, conjugate elements have the same length. Therefore l(v)=l(x^{m+1}c^{k-1})=f(m+1,k-1). Substituting the formulas for l(u) and l(v) into the inequality from Lemma 3 gives f(m,k)<= (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Fix x,y in G and set c:=[x,y]. Define f : Z x Z -> R by f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). Then for every m,k in Z, f(m,k) <= (f(m-1,k)+f(m+1,k-1))/2.
source.label: Lemma 4
